source_metadata.title_raw: Heather L. Stroud - A-18-259 - January 7, 2018 - South Lake Tahoe

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Heather L. Stroud 
City Attorney 
City of South lake Tahoe 
I 901 Airport Road 
South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-259 
Dear Ms. Stroud: 
January 7, 2018 
This letter.responds to your request for advice on behalf of South Lake Tahoe City 
Councilmember Cody Bass regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions 
of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict 
of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
The Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct. (Regulation 
18329(b)(8)(A).) Accordingly, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any past 
conduct, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. 
QUESTIONS 
Given that Councilmember Bass has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to 
forthcoming governmental decisions relating to a proposed ordinance that would govern zoning and 
permitting of cannabis businesses within the City (the "Cannabis Ordinance") due to his financial 
interest in Tahoe Wellness Cooperative, Inc. ("TWC"), a medical cannabis dispensary business 
located within the City, does the Act permit the Councilmember to: 
(1) Discuss the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City staff or 
elected officials outside of a noticed ·public meeting under the "appearance as a member of the 
general public" exception set forth in Regulation 18704( d)(2)? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-259 
Page No. 2 
(2) Engage the services of an agent, attorney, or consultant to discuss the Cannabis 
Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City staff or elected officials outside of a noticed 
public meeting pursuant to that exception? 
(3) Make statements on social media regarding the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing 
regulations under the "public speaking" exception set forth in Regulation 18704( d)( 4 )? 
CONCLUSIONS 
(1) & (2) No. The Act does not permit the Councilmember or his agent, attorney, or 
consultant to discuss the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City staff or 
elected officials outside of a noticed public meeting because the "appearance as a member of the 
general public" exception does not apply outside of a noticed public meeting. 
(3) Yes. The Act permits the Councilmember to make widely available public posts on 
social media regarding the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations under the "public 
speaking" exception. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are the City Attorney for the City of South Lake Tahoe and the authorized 
representative of newly elected South Lake Tahoe City Councilmember Cody Bass. The City will 
soon consider whether to enact the Cannabis Ordinance, which would govern zoning and permitting 
of cannabis businesses within the City. The Councilmember is the sole owner ofTWC, and TWC is 
the only medical cannabis dispensary business currently operating within the City. The 
Councilmember has a direct investment in TWC worth $2,000 or more and holds a position of 
management with the business. 
At its October 16, 2018 meeting, the City Council rescinded certain City regulations then 
governing zoning and permitting of cannabis businesses within the City, and directed City staff to 
prepare the Cannabis Ordinance for its consideration at a future meeting. Among other things, the 
Cannabis Ordinance would increase the number and types of permits allowed for various cannabis 
businesses, remove the existing requirement that no more than one cannabis business be permitted 
per lot or parcel, and establish a public safety impact mitigation fee on cannabis businesses. The 
Cannabis Ordinance would also permit TWC to temporarily expand its land use to include adult 
retail cannabis sales for three years, at which point TWC would be required to have obtained all 
City licenses and permits necessary to continue operating. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial 
interests distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) 


File No. A-18-259 
Page No. 3 
The Councilmember's Financial Interests in Decisions Relating to the Cannabis Ordinance 
Section 87103 identifies an official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying 
conflict of interest under the Act. The analysis set forth in your request for advice indicates that 
Councilmember Bass has a business interest in TWC under Section 87103(a) because he has a 
direct investment in TWC worth $2,000 or more, and under Section 87103(d) because he holds a 
management position with TWC. We agree that the Councilmember has a business interest in TWC 
with respect to decisions relating to the Cannabis Ordinance based on the facts presented. 
Additionally, the Councilmember has an interest in his personal finances and those of 
immediate family members urider Section 87103, and a source of income interest in TWC or any 
customer of TWC under Section 87103(c) if the Councilmember aggregates $500 or more from that 
source of income in the 12 months prior to the decisions.2 
The Act Prohibits the Councilmember from Taking Part in Decisions Relating to the Cannabis 
Ordinance 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that: a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701 (b) sets forth the foreseeability 
standard applicable to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and 
provides that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on 
an official's interest in a business entity, including a business entity that is a source of income. (See 
Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Under Regulation 18702.l(b), a decision's effect on a business entity is 
material if a reasonably prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably 
foreseeable ,that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business 
entity. 
TWC is currently the only medical cannabis dispensary permitted to operate within the City. 
Among other things, the Cannabis Ordinance would permit TWC to expand its land use to include 
adult retail cannabis sales without requiring TWC to obtain the City licenses and permits necessary 
to continue operating for three years. This would provide TWC a substantial competitive advantage 
against others trying to obtain a permit for adult retail cannabis sales from the City. Therefore, even 
assuming that TWC is not explicitly involved in the decisions, it is reasonably foreseeable that 
decisions relating to the Cannabis Ordinance will contribute to a change in the value of TWC under 
Regulation 18702.1 (b ). Accordingly, the Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those 
2 Section 82030(a) provides that an individual's income includes a pro rata share of any income of any 
business entity in which the individual owns a 10-percent or greater interest. The Councilmember is the sole owner of 
TWC. Therefore, the Councilmember would have a source of income interest in any customer ofTWC ifhe aggregates 
$500 or more in income from that customer in the 12 months prior to the decisions. 


File No. A-18-259 
Page No. 4 
decisions.3 Because discussions between the Councilmember, or his agent, attorney or consultant, 
and City staff about decisions relating to the Cannabis Ordinance would likely result in a prohibited 
attempt to influence those decisions by the Councilmember, the Act prohibits him from taking part 
in those discussions, unless an exception applies. 
"Appearance as a Member of the General Public" Exception 
Regulation 18704 defines "making," "participating in making," and "using official position 
to attempt to influence" a decision. Regulation 18704( d)(2) provides a limited exception to those 
definitions that permits an otherwise disqualified official to appear before his or her agency in the 
course of its prescribed governmental function as a member of the general public on matters solely 
related to the official's personal interests. The exception applies to a "business entity owned entirely 
by the official, members of his or her immediate family, or the official and members of his or her 
immediate family." (Regulation 18704(d)(2)(B).) 
The Councilmember has a "personal interest" in TWC because he is the sole owner of 
TWC. Therefore, with respect to the Cannabis Ordinance, the Councilmember may speak as a 
member of the general public regarding his personal interest in TWC pursuant to this exception. 
To invoke the exception, the Councilmember must follow the procedures set forth in 
Regulation 18707(a)(3)(C). Specifically, the Councilmember must publicly identify each type of 
financial interest he holds that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest, along with 
specified details about each interest. The identification must be made after the announcement of the 
agenda item to be discussed or voted on, but before the discussion or vote commences. (Willkins 
Advice Letter, No. A-18-227.) The Councilmember must also recuse himself from voting on the 
matter, leave the dais to speak from the same area as the members of the public, and limit his 
remarks solely to his personal interest. Furthermore, the exception would only apply if the 
Councilmember makes it clear that he is not acting in his official capacity when he appears as a 
member of the general public. (Willkins Advice Letter, supra; Albano Advice Letter, No. A-17-208; 
McHugh Advice Letter, No. I-98-324.) The Councilmember, however, may listen to public 
discussion and deliberations on the matter from the same area as other members of the public. 
Your first question is whether the Councilmember may discuss the Cannabis Ordinance or 
its implementing regulations with City staff or elected officials outside of a noticed public meeting 
pursuant to this exception. The exception is interpreted narrowly to only apply when the official's 
agency is holding a noticed public meeting (Albano Advice Letter, supra; Pierik Advice Letter, No. 
I-16-040; Miller Advice Letter, No. A-15-229). Therefore, the exception would not permit the 
Councilmember to discuss the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City staff 
or elected officials outside of a noticed public meeting. 
You have also asked whether the Councilmember may engage the services of an agent, 
attorney, or consultant to discuss the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City 
staff or elected officials outside of a noticed public meeting under this exception. We note that the 
3 We do not analyze whether the decisions relating to the Cannabis Ordinance would have a disqualifying 
effect on any of the Councilmember's other interests because we have already determined that it is reasonably 
foreseeable that those decisions will have a disqualifying effect on his business interest in TWC. 


File No. A-18-259 
Page No. 5 
Councilmember may appear in the same manner as any other member of the public under the 
exception; if a member of the public may appear with an agent, attorney, or consultant, the 
Councilmember may also appear with an agent, attorney, or consultant. (Hansen Advice Letter, No. 
A-15-172.) The Councilmember's engagement of an agent, attorney, or consultant, however, would 
not affect the exception's inapplicability outside a noticed public meeting. Therefore, the exception 
also would not pennit an agent, attorney, or consultant of the Councilmember to discuss the 
Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City staff or elected officials outside of a 
noticed public meeting. 
"Public Speaking" Exception 
Regulation 18704(d)(4) sets forth the "public speaking" exception, another exception to the 
to the Act's definitions of "making," "participating in making," and "using official position to 
attempt to influence" a decision. Your final question is whether the Councilmember may make 
statements on social media regarding the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations 
pursuant to this exception. 
The "public speaking" exception permits an otherwise disqualified official to communicate 
with the general public or media. In interpreting this exception, we have previously advised that an 
official may express his or her opinion to reporters and media outlets by writing a newspaper article 
or appearing on a radio program regarding the matter in which the official has the disqualifying 
interest. (Edelen Advice Letter, No. 1-07-059.) We have also advised that an official is free to 
discuss a project with the press, friends, neighbors, or other members of the community, even if the 
official does so to rally support or opposition of an action, unless those individuals are members, 
officers, employees, or consultants of the official's agency. (Mizrahi Advice Letter, No. A-12-036.) 
An official's use of social media to make a widely available public post regarding a matter 
in which he or she has a disqualifying conflict of interest is analogous to an official authoring a 
newspaper article or appearing on a radio program regarding such a matter, or discussing such a 
matter with members of the community. We caution, however, that the post must be widely 
available, and may not be permitted if the official has a limited number of followers or subscribers. 
Moreover, the official may not directly message City members, officers, employees, or consultants. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
#ll(f.{jffe( 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

Given that Councilmember Bass has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to 
forthcoming governmental decisions relating to a proposed ordinance that would govern zoning and

interest in Tahoe Wellness Cooperative, Inc. ("TWC"), a medical cannabis dispensary business

general public" exception set forth in Regulation 18704( d)(2)?(2) Engage the services of an agent, attorney, or consultant to discuss the Cannabis 
Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City staff or elected officials outside of a noticed 
public meeting pursuant to that exception? 
(3) Make statements on social media regarding the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing 
regulations under the "public speaking" exception set forth in Regulation 18704( d)( 4 )?
sections.conclusion:
(1) & (2) No. The Act does not permit the Councilmember or his agent, attorney, or 
consultant to discuss the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations with City staff or

general public" exception does not apply outside of a noticed public meeting. 
(3) Yes. The Act permits the Councilmember to make widely available public posts on 
social media regarding the Cannabis Ordinance or its implementing regulations under the "public 
speaking" exception.
